chosen_title: PPF for Your Minor Child — The Shared ₹1.5L Cap Most Parents Miss
caption:
Most parents think opening a PPF for their child gives them extra ₹1.5L of investment room. It doesn't.

The rule is clear: your own PPF account and all PPF accounts of your minor children (where you're the guardian) share ONE combined annual limit of ₹1.5 lakh. Not ₹1.5L each — ₹1.5L total.

Here's the trap (illustration): Ravi maxes his own PPF at ₹1.5L, then deposits ₹1L in his daughter's account 'for her future.' That ₹1L earns zero interest. At 7.1% p.a., that's ₹7,100 quietly lost in Year 1 alone. The money is eventually returned — but without a single rupee of growth.

The silver lining: deposits in your child's PPF DO count toward YOUR Section 80C deduction — just within the shared ₹1.5L cap. Splitting between accounts is smart. Exceeding is not.

What to do: Before your next PPF deposit, add up all contributions across your account and your children's accounts this financial year.

Not sure how to split it? WhatsApp us — 15 minutes and it's sorted.

Note: general information, not professional advice — talk to us for your specific case.

Source: PPF Scheme 1968, Rule 3(1); PPF interest rate per Ministry of Finance notification.

slides.label: MONEY MYTH
slides.image_prompt: Indian father at a bank branch desk handing a small PPF passbook to his young daughter, around 8 years old, both smiling, warm afternoon sunlight through tall windows, a quiet moment of parental pride
slides.headline: Opening PPF for your child? That ₹1.5L limit is shared — not doubled.

slides.label: MYTH vs FACT
slides.myth: My PPF and my child's PPF each have their own ₹1.5L annual limit.
slides.truth: All PPF accounts — yours and your minor child's — share ONE combined ₹1.5L cap.

slides.label: REAL TALK
slides.headline: Overshoot that cap and the extra money earns absolutely nothing.

slides.label: THE NUMBERS
slides.body: Interest lost on ₹1L excess PPF deposit — one year at 7.1% p.a.

slides.label: REAL TALK
slides.image_prompt: Close-up of two PPF passbooks placed side by side on a wooden desk — one adult-sized, one smaller with a child's name — next to a calculator displaying zero, soft diffused window light, muted tones
slides.headline: Two passbooks. One limit. Split it right.
slides.caption: Excess deposits earn nothing — not even a rupee.

slides.label: TAX PLANNING
slides.title: Before you deposit, know this
points: Combined cap: your PPF + all minor children's PPFs
Excess earns zero interest — returned as-is
Child's deposit still qualifies for your 80C
Split the ₹1.5L wisely — don't exceed it
